names.scientific_name: Polyglycerol esters of polycondensed fatty acids from castor oil
names.japanese_name: ポリグリセリンポリリシノレート
names.japanese_label_name: ポリグリセリン脂肪酸エステル

natural_occurrence.description: Synthetic emulsifier made from castor oil and polyglycerol. Extremely effective at reducing chocolate viscosity, allowing manufacturers to use less expensive cocoa butter. This is the primary reason PGPR is so ubiquitous in mass-market chocolate - it's a significant cost-saving ingredient that allows chocolate to flow better during manufacturing.

manufacturing.description: Produced by esterifying polyglycerol with condensed ricinoleic acid derived from castor bean oil. The resulting compound is an excellent emulsifier for chocolate systems.

eu.conditions: Maximum levels vary by food category; widely permitted in chocolate products

usa.notes: GRAS status; widely used in chocolate manufacturing

japan.japanese_regulation_name: ポリグリセリンポリリシノレート

australia_nz.notes: Approved as emulsifier in chocolate and confectionery

canada.reference: Lists of Permitted Emulsifying, Gelling, Stabilizing or Thickening Agents
canada.notes: Permitted in chocolate and confectionery products

regulatory_history.event: JECFA evaluates PGPR and establishes ADI
EU regulation codifies E476 in Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008
EFSA and JECFA re-evaluate and confirm ADI of 7.5 mg/kg bw/day

commonly_found_in: Mass-market chocolate bars (Cadbury, Hershey's, Nestle, etc.)

adi_context.source_note: Based on JECFA and EFSA ADI of 7.5 mg/kg bw/day. PGPR is found in almost all mass-market chocolate because it reduces viscosity and allows manufacturers to use less cocoa butter, significantly reducing production costs. Premium chocolate brands often avoid PGPR and use only cocoa butter.
